stage.name: Checkpoint 03: order the pipeline spine
stage.description: A choice-only Unit 03 checkpoint that tests stage ordering, frontend/decode/backend responsibilities, hazard reasoning, and commit precision.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

content.title: Checkpoint 03: order the pipeline spine
content.subtitle: Before moving into deeper frontend, scoreboard, and memory topics, prove that the architecture trunk is stable.
content.thesis: A learner is ready for deeper CVA6 reading when stage responsibilities are precise, ordered, and source-grounded.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

content.speakerNotes: This checkpoint closes Unit 03. The purpose is to prove that the pipeline spine is now stable. You should be able to order the six stages, place the frontend and decode responsibilities, explain issue and execute without collapsing them, and describe why commit is the boundary for precise architectural state.

actions.text: This checkpoint closes Unit 03. The purpose is to prove that the pipeline spine is now stable. You should be able to order the six stages, place the frontend and decode responsibilities, explain issue and execute without collapsing them, and describe why commit is the boundary for precise architectural state.

scenes.title: The skills Unit 03 should leave behind

content.title: Architecture reading is a placement discipline
content.subtitle: The first job is not knowing every signal. It is placing each fact in the right stage.

items.title: Order the stages
items.body: PC Generation, Fetch, Decode, Issue, Execute, Commit.

items.title: Read FRONTEND cleanly
items.body: Separate next-PC selection from fetch delivery and queueing.

items.title: Explain decode output
items.body: Decoded instructions become scoreboard-ready backend records.

items.title: Separate completion from retirement
items.body: Execute can produce results before commit exposes architectural state.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: architecture and modules
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The architecture block diagram groups FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, CONTROLLER, and CSRFILE as the core reading map.

content.speakerNotes: Unit 03 should leave behind a placement discipline. You do not need every RTL signal yet. You do need the order. You need FRONTEND as PC selection plus fetch delivery. You need decode as the point where instruction bits become a backend record. And you need the difference between completion and retirement.

actions.text: Unit 03 should leave behind a placement discipline. You do not need every RTL signal yet. You do need the order. You need FRONTEND as PC selection plus fetch delivery. You need decode as the point where instruction bits become a backend record. And you need the difference between completion and retirement.

content.title: One clean pass through the core
content.subtitle: Use this route when you explain CVA6 to someone opening the design docs for the first time.

steps.title: Choose
steps.body: PC Generation chooses the candidate address stream.

steps.title: Deliver
steps.body: Fetch requests, realigns, queues, and hands instruction work to decode.

steps.title: Interpret
steps.body: Decode expands and translates instruction bits into tracked backend meaning.

steps.title: Admit
steps.body: Issue checks hazards, operands, and readiness.

steps.title: Produce
steps.body: Execute runs functional paths and produces results or control decisions.

steps.title: Retire
steps.body: Commit updates visible state in order and handles precise exceptions.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the one-page route. Choose, deliver, interpret, admit, produce, retire. Those verbs are not official stage names, but they help you remember the responsibility of each official stage. When you teach CVA6, this route keeps the explanation from becoming a pile of module names.

actions.text: Here is the one-page route. Choose, deliver, interpret, admit, produce, retire. Those verbs are not official stage names, but they help you remember the responsibility of each official stage. When you teach CVA6, this route keeps the explanation from becoming a pile of module names.

content.title: Professional explanations preserve boundaries
content.subtitle: Most bad architecture summaries are not false; they are too blurry to be useful.

columns.title: Weak explanation
columns.body: CVA6 fetches, decodes, executes, and commits instructions. The backend has hazards and exceptions.

columns.title: Professional explanation
columns.body: FRONTEND chooses and delivers instruction work, decode builds scoreboard-ready records, issue gates hazards and operands, execute produces results, and commit retires precise state.

content.takeaway: More words are not the goal. Sharper boundaries are the goal.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ISSUE_STAGE and scoreboard
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The issue path tracks RAW, WAW, and structural hazards, uses forwarding when possible, issues instructions in order, and can still receive out-of-order functional-unit results.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The commit stage updates architectural state, commits stores, writes CSR and register-file state, manages stalling/flushing, and handles precise exceptions at commit.

content.speakerNotes: The professional version is not longer just for fun. It is sharper. FRONTEND chooses and delivers. Decode builds a backend record. Issue gates hazards and operands. Execute produces results. Commit retires precise state. Once boundaries are clear, later lessons can go much deeper without sounding like a fog machine.

actions.text: The professional version is not longer just for fun. It is sharper. FRONTEND chooses and delivers. Decode builds a backend record. Issue gates hazards and operands. Execute produces results. Commit retires precise state. Once boundaries are clear, later lessons can go much deeper without sounding like a fog machine.

questions.question: Which stage order is correct?

options.label: PC Generation, Fetch, Decode, Issue, Execute, Commit
Fetch, Decode, Commit, Issue, Execute, PC Generation
Decode, Issue, PC Generation, Fetch, Commit, Execute
Commit, Execute, Issue, Decode, Fetch, PC Generation

questions.analysis: The official six-stage order begins with PC Generation and ends with Commit.

questions.question: Which pair best describes FRONTEND?

options.label: PC generation and fetch
Commit and CSR retirement only
Register file and store buffer only
Decode and final memory update

questions.analysis: The design document describes FRONTEND as implementing PC generation and fetch.

questions.question: Which statements belong to decode?

options.label: Compressed instruction expansion can occur before main decode output
Decoded information can become a scoreboard entry
CSR or controller context can affect legality and flush behavior
Stores are finally committed to architectural memory

questions.analysis: Decode handles expansion, semantic output, and context-related exception evidence; final store commitment is commit work.

questions.question: Which issue-stage concerns are real backend concerns?

options.label: RAW dependencies
WAW dependencies
Functional-unit or result-resource readiness
Choosing the official website logo

questions.analysis: Issue checks dependency and structural readiness before admitting work.

questions.question: Why is “out-of-order result” not the same as “out-of-order commit”?

options.label: Results may become available internally before commit retires architectural state in order
Commit always happens before fetch
Decode writes memory side effects
BTB entries are architectural state

questions.analysis: Completion and retirement are different concepts.

questions.question: What makes a CVA6 architecture explanation public-ready?

options.label: It names the stage, the responsibility, and the source-backed boundary
It uses the word pipeline as often as possible
It avoids official documentation
It generalizes every CV32A60X fact to all variants

questions.analysis: Public-ready lessons should be source-grounded and boundary-aware.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ISSUE_STAGE and scoreboard
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The issue path tracks RAW, WAW, and structural hazards, uses forwarding when possible, issues instructions in order, and can still receive out-of-order functional-unit results.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The commit stage updates architectural state, commits stores, writes CSR and register-file state, manages stalling/flushing, and handles precise exceptions at commit.

content.speakerNotes: The checkpoint is strict because Unit 04 will get more detailed. If the learner cannot order the stages or place responsibility, deeper frontend and scoreboard lessons will collapse. Choice-only questions keep the public course locally gradable, which matters for the website experience.

actions.text: The checkpoint is strict because Unit 04 will get more detailed. If the learner cannot order the stages or place responsibility, deeper frontend and scoreboard lessons will collapse. Choice-only questions keep the public course locally gradable, which matters for the website experience.

content.title: The architecture spine is complete
content.subtitle: You can now move from the trunk into deeper branches without losing the map.
takeaways: The six-stage order is stable and source-backed.
Frontend, decode, issue, execute, and commit responsibilities are separated.
Completion, forwarding, hazards, and precise retirement now have clear homes.
content.nextStep: Next unit: a deeper frontend pass on PC generation, prediction, fetch, and redirect behavior.
content.closing: The map is not the territory, but without the map the territory eats your afternoon.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

content.speakerNotes: The architecture spine is complete. You can order the stages, explain the handoffs, and place major responsibilities without overclaiming. The next unit can now go deeper into frontend behavior. That is the benefit of doing this carefully: future details have a home, and the course can become more technical without becoming less clear.

actions.text: The architecture spine is complete. You can order the stages, explain the handoffs, and place major responsibilities without overclaiming. The next unit can now go deeper into frontend behavior. That is the benefit of doing this carefully: future details have a home, and the course can become more technical without becoming less clear.
